What Are Smart Textiles?
========================

Dec 9

Written By [Loomia Technologies Inc](https://www.loomia.com/blog?author=6328cc8d11fa1f2d7fdbf734)

There is a lot of information floating around regarding smart textiles. However, with so many other similar terms floating around—navigating this industry and how the technology works can be well, difficult. We are going to define exactly what a smart textile is, tell you a bit about the history of this technology, the meaning of active versus passive smart textiles, and the various application areas. Read on to (hopefully) clear up any confusion you may have!

![Image 3](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/6328ce4d4e07a431eb701dc0/e85fe405-8766-471f-ba34-7d7954d7bf65/Screenshot+%28415%29.png)

**_Download our E-Book by opting in to our Mailing List for more on E-textiles and Smart Textiles:_**

Name(required)

First Name

 

Last Name

 

Email(required)

 

- [x] Sign up for news and updates 

Submit Submit

### **Defining smart textiles**

Smart textiles, also known as electronic textiles (e-textiles) or smart fabrics, are textiles that contain electronic components and enhance the features of wearables, automobiles, and other products. They are either made into a textile-based product, or created with the intention of being integrated into a textile.

There are some other terms to be made aware of. And while they can be used interchangeably in some cases, we avoid doing so to prevent any further misinformation or confusion. Here are some other words you may see floating around in place of smart textiles:

*   Technical textile

*   Intelligent textile

*   Functional material

*   Functional textile

*   Functional fabric

*   Nano-fabric

*   Electronic fabric

*   Smart fibre

*   Soft circuit

Smart textiles, such as our [Loomia Electronic Layer (LEL)](https://www.loomia.com/lel-for-engineers), can provide a wide variety of functionality to soft goods including heating, lighting, and sensing technology. It is important to note that some smart textile technologies, like the LEL, can be integrated into other types of surfaces such as wood and polycarbonate.

### **History of smart textiles**

Here are some key moments in the history of smart textiles. You can read more [here](https://www.loomia.com/blog/a-brief-history-of-e-textiles-soft-circuits).

*   1600 — Early conductive threads are said to date back to the Elizabethan era when gold threads were woven into garments for a gleaming accent. Now, we often use silver, or nickel threads for conductivity, but the concept of metallic threads has existed for centuries for decorating garments.

*   2007 — Leah Buechley develops the[Lilypad](http://lilypadarduino.org/), a microcontroller made specifically for textiles. (Adafruit later makes its own version called the [Flora](https://www.adafruit.com/products/659))

*   2014 — Dupont presents[their stretchable, conductive ink](http://www.dupont.com/content/dam/dupont/products-and-services/electronic-and-electrical-materials/documents/prodlib/DuPont-Electronic-Inks-for-the-Wearable-World.pdf)at Printed Electronics 2014

*   2014 — The[MIT Biosuit](http://news.mit.edu/2014/second-skin-spacesuits-0918)creates a strong use case for e-textile in industry

*   2015 — [Google’s Project Jacquard](http://www.fastcodesign.com/3046864/meet-project-jacquard-googles-plan-to-turn-all-your-clothes-into-a-touchscreen)directs tech eyes to e-textiles at Google I/O

*   2015-today — Studios such as[Wearable Experiments](http://wearableexperiments.com/),[Interwoven](http://www.getinterwoven.com/)and[The Crated](http://www.thecrated.com/) create practices around e-textiles and making invisible wearable tech. Loomia is born.

### **Passive vs. active smart textiles**

![Image 4](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/6328ce4d4e07a431eb701dc0/a6ac2be2-2a08-494e-a72d-cea2bae58a62/c7-Loomia_r2_095A8319.jpg)

![Image 5](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/6328ce4d4e07a431eb701dc0/1669996418476-8Q97HLJE7XKG7CW2TNM7/unsplash-image-YtgngF3_178.jpg)

Image: Unsplash

There are two main types of smart textile technology; passive and active. Often deemed the “first generation” of smart textiles, passive smart textiles are those that have functionality beyond that of a traditional woven textile. However, let it be known that passive textiles generally do not adapt as a result of the information they sense. In other words, [the textile is not altered when environmental conditions change](https://www.omicsonline.org/open-access/smart-textiles-and-nanotechnology-a-general-overview-2165-8064.1000181.php?aid=40254). Take for example, a cooling fabric that can help to regulate your temperature; but it does not actively provide coolness. The fabric simply helps you to evaporate liquid more quickly due to the construction of the textile. The same goes for apparel and other goods with UV protective, antimicrobial, and conductive/anti-static nanotechnologies.

**Here are some examples of passive smart textile products:**

*   [Cosmo](https://cosmofabric.net/technology/value-added-components/microban/) - A leader in fabrics for footwear, Cosmo utilizes Microban technology to add antimicrobial properties to their textiles; resulting in less odor.

*   [Sunbrella](https://www.sunbrella.com/what-is-sunbrella-fabric) - Sunbrella’s outdoor performance fabrics are treated with UV-stabilized pigments to help maintain lasting color and durability under the harsh rays of the sun.

*   [Herculite](https://www.herculite.com/healthcare-fabrics/conductive-fabrics) - A manufacturer of PVC composite textiles, Herculite fabrics are made for applications that require a high level of durability, such as military, healthcare, and marine, using their unique weaving technology.

![Image 6](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/6328ce4d4e07a431eb701dc0/0d70f85c-4473-4c91-b96b-9179530d61b7/0x0.jpeg)

Active smart textiles are those that adapt and change their functionality in response to changes in the external environment or in response to a user input. These materials may change shape, store and regulate heat, and be applied to a wide range of flexible applications. While passive textiles generally rely on their structure to function, active textiles generally utilize electricity, which can support[actuators and sensors](https://www.omicsonline.org/open-access/smart-textiles-and-nanotechnology-a-general-overview-2165-8064.1000181.php?aid=40254). These electronic components allow the smart fabric to sense touch and temperature, as well as process and interpret a myriad of information pertaining to the external environment. They are a crucial part of smart textile manufacturing.

**Here are some examples:**

*   [Loomia Electronic Layer](https://www.loomia.com/lel-for-engineers) - The Loomia Electronic Layer is a soft, flexible circuit that can enable next-generation products in wearables, automotive, and beyond.

*   [HeiQ Smart Temp](https://www.heiq.com/products/textile-technologies/heiq-smart-temp-thermoregulation/?_gl=1%2A15lantd%2A_up%2AMQ..&gclid=Cj0KCQiA4aacBhCUARIsAI55maG-Mqer0UpHFn8Xn7KM0yzBDqVcUXU_mTfXrdPrVkCDeBvg938eTPAaAgsdEALw_wcB) - HeiQ’s Smart Temp fabric features intelligent thermoregulation technology that is triggered by the body’s temperature, and cools the user down as a result.

*   [Gentherm](https://gentherm.com/en/home) - Gentherm manufactures a few different technologies, each with a different purpose. Their ClimateSense® technology is built on an algorithm that changes the temperature in a vehicle or medical wearable depending on the user’s unique preferences.

### **Key application areas**

There are several applications of passive and active smart textiles. However, for the sake of this particular article, we are going to zero-in on those in wearable technology, outdoor apparel and accessories, and automotive interiors.

#### Passive

The most common application of passive smart textiles is in outdoor apparel and medical wearables. We provided you with the example of a cooling textile already, but these textiles can be used in more ways than one. Here are some other possible functions of passive textiles:

*   **UV protective** - Passive elements, [such as optical brighteners and UV absorbers](http://cdn.intechopen.com/pdfs/12251/InTech-Wowen_fabric_and_ultraviolet_protection.pdf), are added to fibers to create sun-protective properties. When used in combination with other technical components, these materials can be very effective at preventing skin damage and other related conditions that are the result of sun exposure.

*   **Antimicrobial** - Antimicrobial composite coatings are a popular commodity in the textile industry, especially in the manufacturing of activewear. These coatings are effective in preventing the growth of bacteria that is produced from perspiration. While these coatings may be saving your clothes, they are certainly not saving the planet. One would think that the chemicals in the coatings are a concern, but the problem actually lies [in the disposal of these textiles](https://www.womenshealthmag.com/style/a19941548/antimicrobial-activewear-environment/).

#### Active

Active smart textiles have a wide range of capabilities, and possess many traits that passive smart textiles do not. As mentioned earlier, active textiles adapt and change their functionality in response to the external environment. These materials produce an “action” as a result of the information obtained (think of it this way: _active_ equals _action_). The following are just a few examples of what active textiles can do:

*   **Thermoregulation** - Among the most common applications of active textiles is in outerwear; specifically, wearables that regulate the body’s temperature. Earlier this year, we at Loomia produced a heated jacket. Our [LEL (Loomia Electronic Layer)](https://www.loomia.com/our-technology) was integrated directly into the garment, providing [a subtle, yet comfortable climate](https://www.wearabletechnologyinsights.com/articles/16745/loomia-h1-heated-jacket-for-women) for the wearer. This is a particularly exciting development, as this technology could one day be used to help the elderly and those with medical conditions stay warm (and, for a relatively affordable price).

*   **Seat sensing and heating** - The automotive industry is, perhaps, the largest to implement active smart textiles. There are several concepts currently in the development process, but one that stands out most is the [ability to distinguish a car’s driver by their size and weight](https://newatlas.com/bebop-sensors-smart-fabric-car-seats/43350/) through pressure sensors. However, one application that several of the world’s largest car manufacturers (and consumers alike) are particularly fond of is heated seats. Yes, that wonderful setting that warms your soul after shoveling out your car is a product of smart textiles!

The e-textiles/smart textiles world can be rather complex and multifaceted. However, don’t let this intimidate you and keep you from learning more! With that being said, we hope we have made the topic of passive and active smart textiles a bit easier to digest.

Feel free to check out our other blog posts on e-textiles to expand your knowledge even more:

[A Brief History of E-Textiles](https://www.loomia.com/blog/2019/4/20/a-brief-history-of-e-textiles-soft-circuits)

SUMMARY:
Image 5: Smart Textiles With built-in sensors, a smart textile can measure a variety of metrics, from heart rate and body temperature to how much force is being applied to the fabric and what chemicals are in the wearer’s immediate environment. Smart textiles enable precise monitoring of athletic performance, measuring key metrics like heart rate, muscle activity and range of motion to provide actionable insights. Fashion tech company Wearable X launched its line of Nadi X smart yoga pants, woven with built-in sensors and haptic feedback, to guide a user’s yoga practice. Examples of smart fabrics include moisture-wicking athletic wear, temperature-regulating materials, color-changing garments as well as textiles with embedded sensors, like Hexoskin’s biometric monitoring headband and shirt set or MIT’s AI-enabled footwear.

===============

+1

What Are Smart Textiles?
========================

Smart textiles weave tech into traditional fabrics, collecting data about the wearer and their immediate surroundings.

![Image 4: Brooke Becher](https://cdn.builtin.com/cdn-cgi/image/f=auto,w=96,h=96,q=100/https://builtin.com/sites/www.builtin.com/files/2022-09/BecherBrooke_SquareHeadshot_AuthorArchive%20%281%29.jpeg)

 Written by [Brooke Becher](https://builtin.com/authors/brooke-becher)

Published on Nov. 25, 2024

[](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?url=https%3A%2F%2Fbuiltin.com%2Farticles%2Fsmart-textiles&mini=true)

![Image 5: Smart Textiles](https://cdn.builtin.com/cdn-cgi/image/f=auto,fit=cover,w=320,h=200,q=80/https://builtin.com/sites/www.builtin.com/files/2024-11/smart-textile.jpg)

Image: Shutterstock

Smart textiles are wearable fabrics embedded with electronic components, like sensors and actuators, that react to their environment. They can measure stimuli, like temperature, moisture, pressure, light and sound, and respond to an input through dynamic behaviors like changing color or shape, releasing heat or delivering [haptic feedback](https://builtin.com/hardware/haptic-technology).

What Are Smart Textiles?
------------------------

Smart textiles refers to materials with embedded electronics — such as sensors, actuators or conductive fibers — that enable them to detect and respond to external stimuli.

There are two main types of smart textiles: active and passive. Active smart textiles can interact with their environment and respond to stimuli, while passive ones can only monitor these changes without altering their behavior​.

Smart textiles can be used for health monitoring of patients, performance tracking of athletes and operational support of first responders.

An introduction to smart textiles. | Video: Steven the Engineer

How Do Smart Textiles Work?
---------------------------

Smart textiles use specialized fibers, such as conductive yarns, piezoelectric materials or temperature-sensitive fabrics to sense and actuate. With built-in sensors, a smart textile can measure a variety of metrics, from heart rate and body temperature to how much force is being applied to the fabric and what chemicals are in the wearer’s immediate environment.

Once this data is collected, it’s then transmitted to a processing unit. This step can take place within the microprocessors of a wearable device, or outsourced to a smartphone or a connected app, where the information is translated into actionable insights. For example, a device that tracks a runner’s physical activity may relay information to an app that provides feedback on the wearer’s pace, stride or footstriking pattern. An on-body EKG monitor can continually track a patient’s cardiac rhythm, detecting irregularities in the wearer's heart beat. And so on.

If the smart textile is active, the fabric can respond to the data it gathers by triggering a physical change. This could be anything from adjusting temperature, like [heating](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1385894724025270?dgcid=rss_sd_all) or [cooling down](https://news.uchicago.edu/story/uchicago-researchers-invent-new-fabric-reduces-heat) a jacket, or [changing color based on UV exposure](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1385894724045571).

Through this combination of sensing, processing and actuation, smart textiles offer a wide array of applications with their ability to track a wearer’s movements, physiology and surroundings.

Related Reading[Exoskeleton Suits: Real-Life Examples](https://builtin.com/robotics/exoskeleton-suit)

Applications of Smart Textiles
------------------------------

More than just a fashion statement, smart textiles are used across a wide range of real-word applications.

### Healthcare

Smart textiles are used to deliver [more personalized diagnostics](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41928-022-00723-z), monitoring a patient’s vital signs, like heart and respiration rate, with biosensors. Over time, they can track physiological information to flag abnormalities and even catch early warning signs for conditions such as arrhythmias or irregular breathing patterns.

### Sport and Fitness

Smart textiles enable precise monitoring of athletic performance, measuring key metrics like heart rate, muscle activity and range of motion to provide actionable insights. Embedded into [full-body swim suits](https://www.techradar.com/news/this-high-tech-swimsuit-boosts-speed-by-replicating-the-texture-of-a-whales-belly), [yoga pants](https://www.wearablex.com/) and app-connected footwear, water-resistant e-textiles use motion sensors to collect data and analyze biomechanics. This information can then be used to enhance performance or even prevent injuries.

### Military

In the military sector, e-textiles [can be used](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/346311696_Smart_Textiles_for_Defence_Applications) to monitor vital signs, detect injuries, locate soldiers and transmit real-time data to medical and intel teams, as well as detect environmental hazards, such as toxic gasses or radiation, for optimal situational awareness.

### Workplace Safety

Workers wearing biosensors in their garments can be monitored for heat stress or fatigue, while motion sensors can be used to collect data for injury prevention in industries like construction or logistics. Additionally, smart uniforms with location-tracking features enhance on-the-job safety for first responders and those working in particularly hazardous conditions, like miners, enabling rapid response to emergencies.

Related Reading[3 Ways to Protect Your Wearables and Rugged Devices From Cyber Attacks](https://builtin.com/articles/protect-wearables-rugged-devices)

Examples of Smart Textiles
--------------------------

### Hexoskin’s Smart ‘Astroskin’ Shirt

Astronaut David Saint-Jacques explains how this smart shirt monitors his vitals in space. | Video: Canadian Space Agency

This [washable headband and shirt set](https://hexoskin.com/pages/astroskin-vital-signs-monitoring-platform-for-advanced-research) is built with a heart monitor, breath sensors, a pulse oximeter and skin temperature sensors that support real-time biometric monitoring for up to 48 hours at a time. Originally developed for astronauts, Hexoskin’s Astroskin can detect everything from cardiac conditions to sleep quality of the user, while studying the effects of altitude on the body during space missions.

### Nuubo’s Wearable EKG Monitor

A walkthrough on how Nuubo’s EKG monitor helps diagnose cardiac patients. | Video: Xantus Viomedical

Medtech company Nuubo has developed [a cardiac monitoring system](https://www.nuubo.com/en-us/patients/what-is-nuubo) in the form of a vest that can be worn under clothes for up to 30 days. Embedded with vital-tracking electrodes, it records cardiac rhythms continuously during daily activities. Any anomalies are detected quickly, then documented in a report that informs a patient’s treatment upon their next office visit.

### 3DKnITS, an Intelligent Sock by MIT

MIT’s smart sock thermoforms to the user when it senses pressure. | Video: MIT Media Lab

MIT researchers [developed a smart fabric](https://www.media.mit.edu/projects/3dknits/overview/) using advanced knitting machines to create customizable, body-conforming textiles. As the user applies different levels of pressure, sensors in the 3D-shaping fabrics collect information about a user’s biomechanics — like their gait, balance and muscle coordination — for AI-enabled analysis.

### Feel Sound with CuteCircuit’s SoundShirt

This wearable creates a full body sensory experience for hard-of-hearing users. | Video: Cooper Hewitt

The [SoundShirt](https://cutecircuit.com/soundshirt/) is a garment developed by CuteCircuit that allows deaf users to “feel” music. With 28 micro-actuators and haptic sensors integrated into its fabric, the shirt translates sound into vibrations that are felt on the skin, mapping different sound frequencies to various parts of the body. This creates an immersive experience that makes musical performances more inclusive and engaging for those with hearing impairments.

### UCR's Self-Healing Fabric

The self-repairing properties of this material were inspired by Marvel’s comic book character Wolverine. | Video: UCR

Researchers at the University of California, Riverside [created a conductive, self-healing fabric](https://www.universityofcalifornia.edu/news/wolverine-inspired-material) that autonomously repairs itself. After being cut, the material can re-attach itself in under 24 hours at room temperature. Made up of ionic conductors, the material can be electrically activated, and could be used to power artificial hearts and robots.

### Wearable X’s Smart Leggings

These yoga pants keep users in proper form by sending vibrational cues. | Video: Dezeen

Fashion tech company Wearable X launched its line of [Nadi X smart yoga pants](https://www.wearablex.com/pages/how-it-works), woven with built-in sensors and haptic feedback, to guide a user’s yoga practice. The smart leggings work alongside a mobile app to provide vibrational cues that prompt the wearer to adjust their form, enhancing their yoga flow with instruction of proper alignment.

Frequently Asked Questions
--------------------------

### What are examples of smart fabrics?

Examples of smart fabrics include moisture-wicking athletic wear, temperature-regulating materials, color-changing garments as well as textiles with embedded sensors, like Hexoskin’s biometric monitoring headband and shirt set or MIT’s AI-enabled footwear.

### What is the difference between smart textiles and e-textiles?

A smart textile is an umbrella term that encompasses any fabric with functional properties that respond to external stimuli, whereas an e-textile, which is a type of smart textile, refers to materials that are specifically integrated with electronic components, like sensors or circuits.

Do Not Sell/Share My Personal Info
----------------------------------

When you visit our website, we store cookies on your browser to collect information. The information collected might relate to you, your preferences or your device, and is mostly used to make the site work as you expect it to and to provide a more personalized web experience. However, you can choose not to allow certain types of cookies that are considered to be “sales” or “sharing” of personal information for targeted advertising purposes. Click on the different category headings to find out more and change our default settings according to your preference. You cannot opt-out of our First Party Strictly Necessary Cookies as they are deployed in order to ensure the proper functioning of our website (such as prompting the cookie banner and remembering your settings, to log into your account, to redirect you when you log out, etc.). For more information about the First and Third Party Cookies used please follow this link: 

[More information](https://cookiepedia.co.uk/giving-consent-to-cookies)

Allow All
### Sale/Sharing of Personal Information

#### Strictly Necessary Cookies

Always Active

These cookies are necessary for the website to function and cannot be switched off in our systems. They are usually only set in response to actions made by you which amount to a request for services, such as setting your privacy preferences, logging in or filling in forms. You can set your browser to block or alert you about these cookies, but some parts of the site will not then work. These cookies do not store any personally identifiable information.

#### Targeting Cookies

- [x] Targeting Cookies 

These cookies may be set through our site by our advertising partners. They may be used by those companies to build a profile of your interests and show you relevant adverts on other sites. They do not store directly personal information, but are based on uniquely identifying your browser and internet device. If you do not allow these cookies, you will experience less targeted advertising.

#### Performance Cookies

- [x] Performance Cookies 

These cookies allow us to count visits and traffic sources so we can measure and improve the performance of our site. They help us to know which pages are the most and least popular and see how visitors move around the site. All information these cookies collect is aggregated and therefore anonymous. If you do not allow these cookies we will not know when you have visited our site, and will not be able to monitor its performance.

The Latest Breakthroughs in Smart Textile Applications
======================================================

3 Apr 2024 |

[E-Textile & Smart Textile](https://textilesinside.com/markets/e-textile-smart-textile/)

 In 2023, a wave of innovations swept through the smart textile industry, marked by groundbreaking advancements from global research institutions. These developments herald a new era in the integration of technology and textiles, offering transformative potential across various sectors. 

From WSU’s electrically conductive, cotton-like fibres to MIT’s temperature-responsive FibeRobo, innovative breakthroughs are reshaping the landscape of [smart textiles](https://textilesinside.com/e-textile-smart-textile/). These advancements, such as Singapore’s spider-inspired durable fibres and Canada’s heat and electricity-activated materials, highlight the versatility of this rapidly evolving field. These developments not only enhance textile functionality but also open new avenues in healthcare, automotive, and fashion industries, blending traditional craftsmanship with cutting-edge technology.

WSU’s Pioneering Cotton-Like Fibre
----------------------------------

A breakthrough in smart textile innovation emerges from [Washington State University](https://news.wsu.edu/press-release/2023/12/11/new-conductive-cotton-based-fiber-developed-for-smart-textiles/) (WSU), where researchers have developed a single strand of fiber combining the flexibility of cotton with the electric conductivity of polyaniline polymer. Published in the journal Carbohydrate Polymers, their study showcases the potential of these fibers for wearable [e-textiles](https://textilesinside.com/e-textile-smart-textile/). Led by Hang Liu, the WSU team envisions integrating these fibers into apparel as sensor patches with flexible circuits, catering to diverse applications such as hazardous exposure detection for firefighters or health monitoring in everyday clothing.

Overcoming challenges in mixing the conductive polymer with cotton cellulose, the researchers devised a method involving dissolving recycled t-shirt cellulose and the conductive polymer separately before merging them side-by-side. This delicate balance at the interface ensures optimal bonding without compromising conductivity. Supported by the National Science Foundation and the Walmart Foundation Project, this research underscores the transformative role of interdisciplinary collaboration in advancing smart textile technologies.

MIT’s Shape-Changing FibeRobo
-----------------------------

MIT researchers have unveiled [FibeRobo](https://news.mit.edu/2023/shape-shifting-fiber-can-produce-morphing-fabrics-1026), a programmable fiber poised to revolutionize textile manufacturing and wearable technology. This low-cost fiber exhibits remarkable shape-changing capabilities, contracting in response to temperature changes and reverting without embedded sensors or hard components. Compatible with existing textile manufacturing techniques, including weaving looms and industrial knitting machines, FibeRobo offers endless possibilities for adaptive performance wear and smart garments.

By incorporating conductive thread, it can be activated electrically, enabling digital control over fabric form. Jack Forman, the lead author, highlights the fiber’s potential to transform everyday textiles into responsive and dynamic materials, seamlessly integrating functionality into fashion. Through meticulous fabrication processes, the team produced FibeRobo at a fraction of the cost of commercially available alternatives. With applications ranging from adaptive sports bras to compression jackets for pets, this groundbreaking fiber heralds a new era of interactive and versatile textiles. Collaborators from MIT and Northeastern University contributed to this interdisciplinary research, supported by various grants and fellowships.

Singapore’s Spiders-Inspired Soft Fibres
----------------------------------------

Taking inspiration from spiders’ silk-spinning process, researchers at the [National University of Singapore](https://news.nus.edu.sg/nus-researchers-create-method-to-produce-soft-recyclable-fibres/) (NUS) have developed an innovative method to produce soft, recyclable fibers for smart textiles. This method, known as phase separation-enabled ambient (PSEA) spinning, allows for the creation of strong, stretchable, and electrically conductive fibers at room temperature and pressure. By mimicking the natural process of spider silk formation, the team successfully integrated three key properties into the fibers: strength, elasticity, and electrical conductivity.

These fibers, made from a gel solution of polyacrylonitrile (PAN) and silver ions, demonstrate promising applications in smart technology, including interactive gaming gloves and temperature-sensing face masks. Moreover, the environmentally friendly aspect of these fibers lies in their recyclability, as they can be dissolved in a common solvent and reused to produce new fibers. This breakthrough offers a sustainable and efficient solution for the production of functional soft fibers in various smart applications.

Canadian Innovation: Heat and Electricity-Activated Materials
-------------------------------------------------------------

Researchers at the [University of Waterloo](https://uwaterloo.ca/news/media/new-programmable-smart-fabric-responds-temperature-and) have developed a groundbreaking smart material that responds to both heat and electricity, marking the first of its kind to do so. This innovative fabric opens doors to various applications, including self-warming clothing and resilient vehicle bumpers. Made from polymer nano-composite fibers sourced from recycled plastic, the fabric can change color and shape upon stimulation. Dr. Milad Kamkar, a chemical engineering professor at Waterloo, highlights its potential in AI, robotics, and virtual reality experiences.

The fabric’s design blends soft and hard materials, utilizing highly engineered polymer composites and stainless steel in its woven structure. Moreover, the fabric can be activated with lower voltage, enhancing energy efficiency and enabling integration into smaller devices like biomedical tools and environmental sensors. Inspired by biomimicry, the material’s ability to sense and react to environmental stimuli offers possibilities for eco-friendly monitoring applications. Moving forward, researchers aim to enhance the fabric’s shape-memory capabilities for use in robotic systems, envisioning versatile robots capable of carrying and transferring weight efficiently.

UK’s Sustainable Smart Textiles with Integrated Functionality
-------------------------------------------------------------

Researchers from the [University of Cambridge](http://www.eng.cam.ac.uk/news/innovative-wearable-e-textiles-fit-sustainable-circular-economy) advocate for a paradigm shift in e-textile manufacturing towards sustainability, highlighting the need for a transition to unlock innovative designs within a circular economy framework. Embracing the 4R design concept—repair, recycle, replace, reduce—alongside functional fiber production and biofabrication techniques, they propose a revolutionary approach. By integrating these concepts, they aim to address challenges such as high costs and environmental impacts associated with [e-textile](https://textilesinside.com/e-textile-smart-textile/) production.

Their strategy emphasizes material innovations, modular designs, and user-centric approaches to enhance affordability, versatility, and durability. Moreover, they envision a future where e-textiles incorporate self-healing mechanisms, facilitate recycling, and utilize renewable materials, ultimately leading to sustainable and customizable fabric designs. This collaborative effort seeks to redefine e-textile manufacturing, promoting a shift towards decentralized, additive manufacturing platforms for on-site production and repair.

Finnish Ingenuity: Temperature-Reactive Smart Fabrics
-----------------------------------------------------

Researchers at [Aalto University](https://www.aalto.fi/en/news/shrinking-from-the-heat) have developed innovative textiles that undergo shape changes in response to heat, offering designers a plethora of creative possibilities. By utilizing liquid crystalline elastomers (LCEs), which can react to various stimuli, the team has woven these smart materials into fabrics using conventional textile crafting techniques. Collaborating with experts from the University of Cambridge, they explored different weaving patterns and tested how these fabrics respond to heat. The results showed reversible contractions in all LCE fabrics when heated, with each pattern exhibiting a unique response.

Additionally, the team demonstrated a proof-of-concept by weaving LCE yarns with other materials to create a fabric that transforms into a cone when heated and returns to its original shape upon cooling. This interdisciplinary research showcases the potential of integrating advanced materials with traditional textile methods, paving the way for the development of responsive textiles with diverse applications in fashion, healthcare, and interior design.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Smart textiles are classified into two main types: passive, which monitor environmental conditions without altering, and active, which respond dynamically to stimuli.
Smart textiles find applications in various sectors, including healthcare for monitoring vital signs, sports for performance tracking, and automotive for enhancing user comfort in vehicles.
The smart textiles industry is poised for significant growth, driven by advancements in technology and increasing demand for functional fabrics in diverse markets.
